coredata.dc:title: Diethylenetriamine-β-CD-modified carbon quantum dots for selective fluorescence sensing of Hg2+ and Fe3+ and cellular imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Ju
Yang, Yunhan
Su, Lijiao
Tao, Xin
Zhang, Juntong
Chen, Yan
Yang, Lijuan
coredata.dc:description: Diethylenetriamine-β-cyclodextrin-modified carbon quantum dots (3 N-CQDs) were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid as the carbon source and diethylenetriamine-β-cyclodextrin (3 N-β-CD) as the nitrogen source. The successful preparation of 3 N-CQDs were revealed by infrared absorption spectroscopy, ultraviolet (UV)-visible absorption spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, XRD, XPS, TEM, and TG. Further spectroscopic studies showed that the synthesized carbon quantum dots offered good anti-interference capability. The relative fluorescence quantum yield was 67.2 %. The limits of detection for Hg2+ and Fe3+ were 0.25 µM and 0.57 μM, respectively. Cytotoxicity and imaging studies showed that the prepared carbon quantum dots had low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, and good cellular imaging capability for HeLa cells. They offered fluorescent sensing of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in live cells. Therefore, 3 N-CQDs were ideal fluorescent probes for the detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence sensing
Hg2+
Fe3+
Cellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Calculation of the fluorescence quantum yield
Study on the fluorescence sensing of Hg2+ and Fe3+
anti-interference study on the recognition of Hg2+ and Fe3+ by 3 N-CQDs
Fluorescence responses of 3 N-CQDs to Hg2+ and Fe3+

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21762051 ), Program for Xingdian Talents (Jiaoxue Mingshi), Program for Innovative Research Team (in Science and Technology) in University of Yunnan Province, Science Research Fund of Yunnan Provincial Education Department ( 2022Y462 ), China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR spectra of CA, 3 N-β-CD, and 3 N-CQDs; (b) UV absorption spectra of CA, 3 N-β-CD, and 3 N-CQDs, and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of 3 N-CQDs.
(a) TEM image of 3 N-CQDs. (b) Particle size distribution of 3 N-CQDs.
XPS spectrum of 3 N-CQDs: (a) full-spectrum, (b) C1s spectrum, (c) N1s spectrum, and (d) O1s spectrum.

(a) Fluorescence response of 3 N-CQDs at different pH values; (b) Fluorescence response of 3 N-CQDs at different NaCl concentrations. (c) The fluorescence response of 3 N-CQDs under 365 nm UV lamp for different time.
(a) Fluorescence response curves of 3 N-CQDs with different cations; (b) Fluorescence images of 3 N-CQDs with different cations under 365 nm irradiation.

(a) Curve of change in fluorescence intensity after 0 – 167 μmol·L−1 Hg2+ was added to 3 N-CQDs solution; (inset: the curve of the relationship between change in fluorescence intensity and Hg2+ concentration); (b) Curve of change in fluorescence intensity after 0 – 500 μmol·L−1 Fe3+ was added to the 3 N-CQDs solution (inset: the curve of the relationship between change in fluorescence intensity and Fe3+ concentration).

Histogram of fluorescence intensity of 3 N-CQDs solution after different types of cations were added; green: addition of Hg2+ and then the addition of other ions, orange: addition of Fe3+ and then the addition of other ions.

(a) Zeta potential plots of 3 N-CQDs, 3 N-CQDs + Fe3+, and 3 N-CQDs + Hg2+; (b) HeLa cell viability after 24 h incubation with different concentrations of 3 N-CQDs. (c) UV absorption spectra of Hg2+, 3 N-CQDs, and 3 N-CQDs + Hg2+. (d) UV absorption spectra of Fe3+, 3 N-CQDs, and 3 N-CQDs + Fe3+. (e) FT-IR spectra of 3 N-CQDs, 3 N-CQDs + Hg2+, and 3 N-CQDs + Fe3+.
Fluorescence images of (a) HeLa cells cultured with 3 N-CQDs under excitation at 365 nm and then addition of Hg2+ (c) and Fe3+ (e); (b), (d), and (f) are corresponding cell shapes.
Schematic diagram of the preparation of 3 N-CQDs and the possible mechanism of Hg2+ and Fe3+-induced fluorescence quenching.

head.ce:title: Diethylenetriamine-β-CD-modified carbon quantum dots for selective fluorescence sensing of Hg2+ and Fe3+ and cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Intelligent Supramolecular Chemistry at the University of Yunnan Province, National and Local Joint Engineering Research Center for Green Preparation Technology of Biobased Materials, School of Chemistry & Environment, Yunnan Minzu University, Kunming 650500, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Intelligent Supramolecular Chemistry at the University of Yunnan Province, National and Local Joint Engineering Research Center for Green Preparation Technology of Biobased Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Intelligent Supramolecular Chemistry at the University of Yunnan Province, National and Local Joint Engineering Research Center for Green Preparation Technology of Biobased Materials, School of Chemistry & Environment, Yunnan Minzu University, Kunming 650500, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Diethylenetriamine-β-cyclodextrin (3N-β-CD)-modified carbon quantum dots were synthesized for the first time via a one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid as the carbon source and diethylenetriamine-β-cyclodextrin as the nitrogen source. The synthesized carbon quantum dots were used as fluorescent probes for the selective detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in an aqueous solution and live cell imaging application on Hela cancer cell line.

Diethylenetriamine-β-cyclodextrin-modified carbon quantum dots (3 N-CQDs) were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid as the carbon source and diethylenetriamine-β-cyclodextrin (3 N-β-CD) as the nitrogen source. The successful preparation of 3 N-CQDs were revealed by infrared absorption spectroscopy, ultraviolet (UV)-visible absorption spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, XRD, XPS, TEM, and TG. Further spectroscopic studies showed that the synthesized carbon quantum dots offered good anti-interference capability. The relative fluorescence quantum yield was 67.2 %. The limits of detection for Hg2+ and Fe3+ were 0.25 µM and 0.57 μM, respectively. Cytotoxicity and imaging studies showed that the prepared carbon quantum dots had low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, and good cellular imaging capability for HeLa cells. They offered fluorescent sensing of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in live cells. Therefore, 3 N-CQDs were ideal fluorescent probes for the detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury ion (Hg2+) is one of the heavy metal ions that can cause severe pollution. It cannot be degraded and can exist for a long time. It easily enriches and accumulates in the food chain, posing a huge health risk to humans, animals, and plants, thus becoming an object of attention [1–3]. Common Hg2+ analysis techniques include cold-vapor atomic fluorescence spectrometry (CV-AFS) [4], cold vapor atomic absorption spectrometry (CV-AAS) [5], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICPMS) [6], ultraviolet spectroscopy, and X-ray absorption spectroscopy [7]. However, these instruments are expensive, complicated, and difficult to transport. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop fast and reliable methods for the detection of Hg2+.

Iron is an essential trace element in the human body, but it can be toxic at high concentrations. Iron ions (Fe3+) are a key component maintaining red blood cell metabolism. It is related to hematopoietic function in the liver, bone marrow, and other target organs. If the human body lacks or contains excessive Fe3+ it can cause illnesses such as anemia, liver diseases, heart disease, and malignant tumors [8,9]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to establish a rapid, sensitive, and selective method for the detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in aqueous solutions. versus traditional detection methods, fluorescent probe methods have received increasing attention for environmental monitoring, and bioimaging, due to their simplicity, rapidity, low cost, and low detection limits.

Based on their unique optical properties, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted the widespread attention of researchers who utilize their good fluorescence to detect heavy metal ions in environmental samples. CQDs generally refer to graphene nanoparticles with a size between 0 and 10 nm [10,11]. They are a new type of zero-dimensional fluorescent nanomaterials [12]. versus quantum dots containing heavy metals, CQDs offer good water solubility, low cytotoxicity, good stability, convenient preparation, and good biocompatibility [13,14]. They have great application potential in drug delivery, sensing, labeling, bioimaging, and photoelectrochemistry [15–24].

Since their discovery in 2004, CQDs have received increasingly more attention and expectations [25,26], and their synthesis methods and applications have also become research hotspots. However, undoped or unfunctionalized CQDs do not show significantly better fluorescence properties than traditional fluorescent materials. Therefore, researchers have started to prepare CQDs with novel structures and improved fluorescence properties via doping and surface modification methods. Doping and surface modification can not only adjust the optical and chemical properties of CQDs but also give the CQDs more functions and expand the application range of CQDs [27,28].

Cyclodextrins (CDs) are second-generation host compounds with a supramolecular chemistry. CD is a macrocyclic compound built of 6 ∼ 12 d-glucopyranose units linked by α-1,4-glycosidic bonds. The macrocyclic compound of β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) is the most studied molecule containing seven glucose units [29]. Because the unique structure of β-CD with a hydrophilic exterior and a hydrophobic interior, it can form host–guest complexes with many compounds [30,31]. CD molecules contain many hydroxyl groups, and therefore, β-CD can be bonded to the surface of carbon quantum dots to stabilize carbon quantum dots, enhance their water solubility, endow them with better optical properties [32], and allow them to be applied to the adsorption and removal of pollutants [33,34].

Herein, for the first time, diethylenetriamine-β-cyclodextrin (3 N-β-CD)-modified carbon quantum dots were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid as the carbon source and diethylenetriamine-β-cyclodextrin as the nitrogen source (Scheme 1 ). The synthesized carbon quantum dots were used as fluorescent probes for the selective detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in an aqueous solution, and their fluorescence activity and biocompatibility were studied further. The results showed that the prepared carbon quantum dots 3 N-CQDs had a wide pH detection range, strong salt tolerance, and low cytotoxicity. They could be used for bioimaging in live cells and fluorescence sensing for Hg2+ and Fe3+ in live cells. This method has advantages in terms of selectivity and simple operation. This is a new type of ideal fluorescent sensing probe.

Citric acid (CA) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were obtained from Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Co., ltd.; acetone was obtained from Chongqing Chuandong Chemical Co., ltd.; β-cyclodextrin, p-toluenesulfonyl chloride, and diethylenetriamine were obtained from Shanghai Titan Technology Co., ltd.; LiCl·H2O, AgNO3, Cs2CO3, KCl, NaCl, NH4Cl, SnCl2·2H2O, ZnCl2, CaCl2, Cu(NO3)3·3H2O, MgCl2·6H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, Pb(NO3)2, CdCl2, BaCl2·2H2O, FeCl2·4H2O, NiCl2·6H2O, SrCl2·6H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, Hg(NO3)2·H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, Bi(NO3)3·5H2O were obtained from Shanghai Titan Technology Co., ltd.; dialysis bags (molecular weight cutoff = 3500 Da and 300 kDa) were purchased from American Spectrumlabs; Deionized water (pH = 6.0 – 7.0), all solutions were prepared from deionized water unless otherwise specified; all reagents used in the experiment were of analytical reagent grade without further purification or treatment.

Morphologies of 3 N-CQDs were investigated using a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (Point resolution: 0.25 nm; Line resolution: 0.102 nm, Accelerated voltage: 80 kV). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were collected by an Agilent 8453 spectrophotometer (Light source: Deuterium lamp and tungsten lamp, wavelength range: 190 – 1100 nm). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured using a NICOLET-IS10 spectrometer (Potassium bromide tablet method). Powder X-ray diffraction spectra were recorded using a Bruker D8 ADVANCE A25X (Working voltage: 40 kV, working current: 40 mA, sweep speed: 12°/ min, 2ɵ = 5° to 40°). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was investigated using a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha instrument (Working voltage: 12 kV, filament current: 6 mA, full spectrum scanning: pass energy 100 eV, step length 1 eV; narrow spectrum scanning: pass energy 50 eV, step size 0.1 eV). TG measurements were performed on a NETZSCH STA2500. (Heating range: 30 °C to 800 °C, heating rate 20 °C / min, nitrogen atmosphere). Zeta potentials were measured using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90 zeta potential analyzer. (The potential was directly measured after ultrasound for 5 min). The cells were imaged using a Nikon inverted fluorescence microscope. The fluorescence lifetime was determined using an Edinburgh FLS1000 transient fluorometer (Light source: 340 – EPLED, detection wavelength: 437 nm, step length: 1 nm, channel: 1024). All fluorescence spectra were recorded using a CARY ECLIPS spectrofluorometer with a 10-nm slit width for excitation and 5-nm slit width for emission.

3 N-β-CD was synthesized according to the literature with β-cyclodextrin as raw materials through a two-step reaction [35]. Pale yellow solid, 70 % yield. 1H NMR (400 MHz, D2O) δ 5.07 (s, 7H), 3.95 – 3.82 (m, 28H), 3.65 – 3.55 (m, 14H), 2.87 – 2.63 (m, 8H). 13C NMR (100 MHz, D2O) δ 101.8, 101.6, 83.6, 81.1, 80.9, 73.1, 73.0, 71.9, 71.8, 70.3, 60.1, 49.7, 49.4, 47.8, 47.4, 39.43, 39.3, 30.2, 30.2, 29.9, 29.8. (Fig. S2 – S3).

Firstly, we investigated the effect of reaction temperature on the preparation of 3 N-CQDs (Fig. S4). The results showed that there was some differences in fluorescence intensity of 3 N-CQDs prepared at different temperatures (140 °C, 160 °C, 180 °C, 200 °C), and the fluorescence intensity of 3 N-CQDs prepared at 180 °C was relatively better. Therefore, we applied the conditions of the literature for preparation [36]. 1.0 g of CA and 0.5 g of 3 N-β-CD were dispersed in a 25 mL beaker and dissolved in 5 mL of water. They were then reacted at 180 °C for 3 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was filtered with a 0.22 µm filter to remove large particulate matter and insoluble matter, and the resulting solution was transferred to a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 3500 Da. Dialysis was then performed for 24 h. Subsequently, the sample was then transferred to a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 300 kDa, and the dialysis was performed for 24 h to remove precipitates with large particle sizes. Finally, the purified solution was vacuum-dried at 50 °C to obtain a light brown solid powder with a yield of 20 %. It was stored at 4 °C for further characterization and research. In this synthesis process, citric acid was fused or carbonized to form a carbon core. The carboxyl group on the surface of the carbon core and the amino group of 3 N-β-CD were further bonded to stabilize and modify the carbon core, which formed the carbon quantum dot.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of the fluorescence quantum yield
ce:section.ce:para:
The relative fluorescence quantum yields of 3 N-CQDs were determined using quinine sulfate as a reference [37]. The quinine sulfate was dissolved in 0.1 mol/L sulfuric acid solution, and calculations were conducted according to the following equation:
(2.1)
∅ X
=
∅ R
×
n X
2
n R
2
×
A R
×
D X
A X
×
D R
( A < 0.05 )
where ØX is the fluorescence quantum yield of 3 N-CQDs, ØR is the fluorescence quantum yield of quinine sulfate, D is the integral area of the fluorescence emission spectra of the two at the same excitation wavelength, and n and A are the refractive index and absorbance of the solution, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the fluorescence sensing of Hg2+ and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: The 3 N-CQDs were used for the selective recognition of Hg2+ and Fe3+. A quantum dot stock solution with a concentration of 10 mg·mL−1 and an ionic stock solution with a concentration of 10 mmol·L−1 were prepared, and the interaction between 3 N-CQDs and cations was recorded by fluorescence spectroscopy. In a color comparison tube, 45 µL of 3 N-CQDs was added via a pipette. The sample was then diluted to 3 mL, and the fluorescence intensity was measured and served as a control group. Similarly, ultrapure water and 3 N-CQDs solution were added into 22 10-mL colorimetric tubes, and then 22 types of cation solutions (namely Li+, Ag+, Cs+, K+, Na+, NH4 +, Sn2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Ba2+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Sr2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, and Bi3+) were added. The fluorescence response of the mixed solution was measured to evaluate the selectivity of 3 N-CQDs to Hg2+ and Fe3+. In the mixed solution, the concentration of quantum dots was 0.15 mg·mL−1, and the concentration of other cations was 160 μmol·L−1. The detection limits for the two ions were investigated by sequentially increasing the concentrations of Hg2+ and Fe3+. In a solution of 3 N-CQDs of known concentration (0.15 mg·mL−1), 2 µL of Hg2+ was added each time, and the fluorescence spectra were measured and recorded under similar conditions. Similarly, 6 µL of Fe3+ was added each time, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded. The cumulative ion volume added here was negligible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: anti-interference study on the recognition of Hg2+ and Fe3+ by 3 N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Next, an anti-interference experiment was designed to study the anti-interference capability of 3 N-CQDs in recognizing Hg2+ and Fe3+. In 20 colorimetric tubes, 45 µL of the above quantum dot solution and 48 µL of Hg2+ were added, and 48 µL of other cation solutions (except Fe3+) was added followed by adding ultrapure water to prepare a solution of 3 mL. The fluorescence intensity was recorded under the test conditions, and the measurements were performed three times in parallel. Using the same method, the anti-interference capability in recognizing iron ions was measured. The concentration of 3 N-CQDs in the final mixed solution was 0.15 mg·mL−1, the concentration of Hg2+ or Fe3+ ions was 160 μmol·L−1, and the concentrations of other cations were all 160 μmol·L−1.

To study the cytotoxicity of the prepared 3 N-CQDs, the biocompatibility with HeLa cells was tested via a cell counting kit-8 (CCK8). In an atmosphere of 5 % carbon dioxide at 37 °C, HeLa cells were cultured in a 96-well medium (containing 10 % fetal bovine serum, 1 % penicillin / streptomycin) at a density of 1 × 104 cells per well for 24 h. Some cells were taken as the control group. The remaining cells were then cultured overnight by adding different concentrations (0, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, and 300 µg·mL−1) of 3 N-CQDs. The medium was removed, and the cells were washed three times with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution (pH = 7.2). Subsequently, 10 μL of a fresh medium of 0.5 mg / mL CCK8 solution was added to each well, and the cells were incubated for 4 h (at 37 °C, 5 % carbon dioxide). The medium containing CCK8 was used as a blank control, and the absorbance of the samples was measured with a microplate reader at 450 nm. The equation for calculating the cell viability is as follows:
(2.2)
C e l l v i a b i l i t y
%
=
Absorbance
treated
-
Absorbance
blank
Absorbance
control
-
Absorbance
blank
× 100 %

To evaluate the feasibility of cellular imaging with 3 N-CQDs, HeLa cells were selected for cellular imaging experiments. HeLa cells were inoculated in 35 mm 24-well plates and cultured in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle medium (DMEM) containing 10 % fetal bovine serum and 1 % penicillin / streptomycin for 24 h before use. The next day, cells were cultured in a fresh medium containing 0.4 mg·mL−1 3 N-CQDs for 6 h (37 °C, 5 % carbon dioxide). The cells were then washed three times with PBS buffer (pH 7.2) to remove the remaining 3 N-CQDs and imaging was conducted with an inverted fluorescence microscope. Subsequently, one part was taken as the control group, and the other part was incubated in the culture medium with 100 μmol·L−1 Hg2+ and 150 μmol·L−1 Fe3+ solution for 30 min. Cell fluorescence imaging was then performed under the irradiation of an additional 365 nm light source. (λex = 365.0 nm and λem = 438.0 nm).

The 3 N-CQDs were characterized by FT-IR absorption spectroscopy, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, X-ray powder diffraction, thermogravimetric analysis, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and fluorescence spectroscopy.

The FT-IR spectra of CA, 3 N-β-CD, and 3 N-CQDs are shown in Fig. 1 a. 3 N-CQDs showed many characteristic absorption bands of 3 N-β-CD with a broad absorption peak at 3419 cm−1, which is associated with OH and NH stretching vibrations [38–40]. The peak at 2932 cm−1 is related to methyl and methylene stretching vibrations [38–41]. The peak at 1595 cm−1 is caused by the NH bending vibration [40]. The peaks at 1155 cm−1, 1079 cm−1, and 1030 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibration peaks of COC on 3 N-β-CD [38–41]. In addition, a new sharp peak related to amide bonds was found at 1701 cm−1, thus indicating that a large number of 3 N-β-CDs may be coated on the surface of 3 N-CQDs with amide bonds [36]. There was no characteristic absorption peak of CA in the spectrum.

The absorption behaviors of CA, 3 N-β-CD, and 3 N-CQDs were measured by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. Fig. 1b shows that the 3 N-CQDs solution had two obvious absorption peaks at 243 nm and 352 nm, and the absorption peak at 243 nm is mainly attributed to the π-π* transition, which is the characteristic UV absorption peak of 3 N-β-CD. The absorption peak at 352 nm may be due to the n-π* transition of the carbonyl group on the surface of 3 N-CQDs [42]. However, CA and 3 N-β-CD had almost no absorption above 352 nm, thus indicating that 3 N-CQDs were successfully prepared.

The XRD results of 3 N-CQDs showed that 3 N-CQDs contained characteristic peaks of 3 N-β-CD but no characteristic peaks of CA, thus, further indicating that 3 N-β-CD was successfully modified on the surface of 3 N-CQDs (Fig. S5). The results of the thermogravimetric analysis showed that the amino and oxygen-containing groups of 3 N-β-CD began to be pyrolyzed from 200 °C, and the weight loss reached 82.22 % when the temperature reached 800 °C (Fig. S6). Similarly, when the temperature reached 800 °C, the mass loss of 3 N-CQDs reached 74.6 %, thus indicating that the surface of the prepared quantum dots contained a large amount of 3 N-β-CD. The above results showed that 3 N-CQDs were successfully prepared.

The TEM images confirmed the size and morphology of the 3 N-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 2 a and 2b, the prepared 3 N-CQDs were approximately spherical particles with an average diameter of 3.4 nm. TEM imaging results showed that the prepared 3 N-CQDs were uniformly dispersed and had very clear lattice fringes, which also illustrated that the properties of carbon quantum dots were similar to graphene.

XPS was used to study the chemical valence state and elemental composition of the surface of 3 N-CQDs. The XPS results indicated that the 3 N-CQDs mainly contained three elements: carbon (59.74 %), nitrogen (3.5 %), and oxygen (36.12 %) (Fig. 3 a). That is, the carbon-based material contained a large number of functional groups of N and O elements. The high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum of 3 N-CQDs (Fig. 3b) showed four fitted peaks, which are attributed to CC (284.8 eV), CO (286.41 eV), CN (285.40 eV), and CO (287.78 eV) [43–45]. The XPS spectrum of N1s (Fig. 3c) contained two fitted peaks, thus indicating the presence of CN (399.88 eV) and NH (401.69 eV) [39]. The spectrum of O1s (Fig. 3d) contained three peaks at 532.65 eV, 531.11 eV, and 534.14 eV, which are associated with CO, CO, and COO– groups, respectively [41]. The XPS results of 3 N-CQDs were consistent with the above characterization results, i.e., the 3 N-β-CD was successfully modified on the surface of 3 N-CQDs.

A quinine sulfate reference was dissolved in 0.1 mol·L−1 sulfuric acid, and the UV absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra of quinine sulfate and 3 N-CQDs were obtained under the same conditions (Fig. S7). The relative fluorescence quantum yield of 3 N-CQDs was calculated to be 67.2 % according to the equation (2.1). The fluorescence quantum yield of 3 N-CQDs was substantially better than other similar research results using citric acid as the carbon source. At the same time, the average fluorescence lifetime of 3 N-CQDs was measured to be 8.71 ns (Fig. S8 and Table S1), which is considerably higher than that of the same type of N-CQDs. This may be due to higher nitrogen content and functionalization of 3 N-β-CD [46].

We further investigated the excitation-dependent emission behavior of 3 N-CQDs, see Supplementary Fig. S9. We noticed that 3 N-CQDs showed an excitation-dependent fluorescence within the excitation wavelength range of 300 to 400 nm with the best excitation wavelength of about 355 nm, indicating that the emission wavelength of 3 N-CQDs could be readily adjusted by modulating the excitation wavelength.
The fluorescence stability of 3 N-CQDs was evaluated by measuring the fluorescence intensity of 3 N-CQDs at pH extremes, high ionic strength, and longtime fluorescent irradiation.

The effect of different pH conditions on the fluorescence intensity of 3 N-CQDs was also evaluated (Fig. 4 a). The results showed that 3 N-CQDs had strong fluorescence intensity over a wide range of pH values = 4.0 – 10.0; the fluorescence intensity was the highest when pH = 8.0. Under strongly acidic conditions, its fluorescence activity was greatly reduced possibly due to the protonation of carboxyl groups on the surface of 3 N-CQDs [47]. The fluorescence intensity of 3 N-CQDs gradually increased with increasing pH. However, the fluorescence intensity of 3 N-CQDs began to decrease when the pH was greater than 10.0, which may be due to the deprotonation of the surface carboxyl groups.

Salt tolerance is an important index to evaluate the stability of 3 N-CQDs. The fluorescence intensity of the solution did not change considerably with gradually increasing ionic intensity around 3 N-CQDs. The fluorescence intensity remained relatively stable even at 2 mol·L−1 NaCl (Fig. 4b). Therefore, these studies showed that 3 N-CQDs possessed relatively good stability. Notably, optical stability is also a necessary index to evaluate the performance of the prepared quantum dots. It could be found that the fluorescence intensity of quantum dot solution decreased slightly under ultraviolet irradiation, and even after continuous irradiation for 50 min, the intensity value only decreased by about 7.5 % (Fig. 4c). This indicated that the nanoparticle had good photostability and was expected to be used in fluorescence analysis and detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence responses of 3 N-CQDs to Hg2+ and Fe3+

ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence intensities of 3 N-CQDs solution (0.15 mg·mL−1) in the presence of Li+, Ag+, Cs+, K+, Na+, NH4 +, Sn2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Ba2+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Sr2+, Mn2+, Bi3+, Hg2+, and Fe3+ were studied at 160 μmol·L−1. The fluorescence intensity of the 3 N-CQDs solution decreased considerably only in the presence of Hg2+ and Fe3+ (Fig. 5 a). The remaining ions did not interact with the CQDs. Of these, the fluorescence response of 3 N-CQDs to Hg2+ was the most significant, thus indicating that the complexation between Hg2+ and 3 N-CQDs was stronger. In the presence of Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity of 3 N-CQDs also changed greatly. The results showed that 3 N-CQDs had good selectivity for both Hg2+ and Fe3+, and the probe was expected to be applied to the detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in real water samples. In addition, considering the effects of the pH value of the sensing system in practical applications, we further explored the detection performance of the probe for Fe3+ and Hg2+ in buffer solution (as shown in Fig. S10). The results showed that the probe could still selectively identify Fe3+ and Hg2+ in 22 common cations in buffer solution (pH = 6).

Fig. 5b is an image of the recognition of Hg2+ and Fe3+ by 3 N-CQDs solution under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation: Only Hg2+ and Fe3+ substantially changed the fluorescence intensity of 3 N-CQDs, and Fe3+ caused the fluorescence intensity of 3 N-CQDs to decrease considerably. Hg2+ could completely quench the fluorescence of 3 N-CQDs.

The detection limits of 3 N-CQDs for Hg2+ and Fe3+ were further investigated. As shown in Fig. 6 a and Fig. 6b, the initial concentration of the 3 N-CQDs solution was 0.15 mg·mL−1. The fluorescence intensity of 3 N-CQDs at 437 nm gradually decreased as the Hg2+ and Fe3+ concentrations increased from 0 to 167 μmol·L−1 and 0 – 500 μmol·L−1, respectively. The insets of Fig. 6a and 6b recorded the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of 3 N-CQDs and the concentrations of Hg2+ and Fe3+, respectively. The linear equations were F0 – F = 7.675 × [Hg2+] – 26.070 (R2 = 0.999) and F0 – F = 3.465 × [Fe3+] + 50.712 (R2 = 0.994). Of these, F and F0 refer to the fluorescence intensity before and after adding Hg2+ or Fe3+, respectively. Hg2+ and Fe3+ concentrations had a good linear relationship with F0 − F in the range of 0 – 87 μmol·L−1 and 0 – 140 μmol·L−1, respectively. The detection limits of Hg2+ and Fe3+ were calculated under the condition of three times the standard deviation of blank samples, and their detection limits were 0.25 μmol·L−1 and 0.57 μmol·L−1, respectively.

In addition, the capability of carbon quantum dots to detect Hg2+ and Fe3+ was analyzed and compared with those reported in the literatures [43,48] as shown in Tables S2, S3 and S4. 3 N-CQDs had a relatively low detection limit, wide linearity range, and high fluorescence quantum yield. These advantages suggest that 3 N-CQDs can serve as an ideal nano-sensor for the detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in water.

Time response is an important factor in evaluating fluorescent probes. The degree of change in fluorescence intensity within 1 h after adding Hg2+ and Fe3+ to the 3 N-CQDs solution was measured (Fig. S11). The time-response study results showed that the probe had no time-response for the recognition of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in aqueous solution, and Hg2+ and Fe3+ could rapidly decrease or quench the fluorescence intensity of 3 N-CQDs within 1 min. The fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution did not change substantially within the next 1 h.

The anti-interference capability is a key indicator to evaluate whether a fluorescent probe can be used for the detection of real samples. Hg2+ or Fe3+ was added to the 3 N-CQDs solution, and then other cations were added to detect the change in the fluorescence intensity of the probe (Fig. 7 ). The study results showed that only a few cations slightly changed the fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution when other cations were added and did not affect the recognition of Hg2+ after adding Hg2+ to the 3 N-CQDs solution. The fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution was generally unchanged when other cations were added after Fe3+ was added to the 3 N-CQDs solution. To further study the anti-interference ability, common organic pollutants such as bisphenol A, hydroquinone and phenol were added to the mixed solution of 3 N-CQDs and the two ions respectively. The experiment results were shown in Fig. S12. The presence of the three organic pollutants did not interfered the detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+. The results showed that 3 N-CQDs could be used as fluorescent probes to recognize Hg2+ and Fe3+ and had good anti-interference capability.

Scheme 1 illustrates the possible mechanism of Hg2+- and Fe3+-induced fluorescence quenching by 3 N-CQDs. Hg2+ and Fe3+ chelate with the amide groups on the surface of 3 N-CQDs, which promote the aggregation and precipitation of 3 N-CQDs, thus resulting in fluorescence quenching. This may be because the electrons of 3 N-CQDs are transferred from the excited state to the empty orbitals of Hg2+ and Fe3+, thus, promoting the radiationless recombination of excitons through an efficient electron transfer process, which leads to substantial quenching of the fluorescence of 3 N-CQDs [49]. To further verify this mechanism, we measured the zeta potentials, UV absorption spectrum and FT-IR spectra of 3 N-CQDs before and after being mixed with Fe3+ and Hg2+: The zeta potential values were − 12.2 mV, −3.92 mV, and − 1.03 mV for solutions of 3 N-CQDs, 3 N-CQDs and Hg2+, and 3 N-CQDs and Fe3+, respectively (Fig. 8 a), thus, indicating that Fe3+ and Hg2+ could be adsorbed on the surface of 3 N-CQDs to increase the potential of 3 N-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 8c, the ultraviolet absorption of 3 N-CQDs solution showed an obvious redshift after the addition of Hg2+. The reason may be that the complexation of Hg2+ and amide bonds resulted in the redshift of absorption peak [50,51]. However, the absorption peak of 3 N-CQDs showed no shift and regeneration after Fe3+ sensing (Fig. 8d), indicating that no new complexes formed. Therefore, the fluorescence response of 3 N-CQDs with Fe3+ may be related to the complexation of Fe3+ and cyclodextrin cavities [52–54]. In addition, the results of FT-IR experiments can further prove the above conclusions. As shown in Fig. 8e, the peak of CO became significantly smaller after 3 N-CQDs and Hg2+ complexation, while the peak of CN on the amide bond at 1384.01 cm−1 became very sharp [38,40]. The peak shape and size of 3 N-CQDs did not change significantly after the addition of Fe3+, indicating that the original chemical bond was not broken. Thus, Fe3+ may be complexed in the cavity of cyclodextrin, while Hg2+ was complexed with amide bonds.

In order to evaluate the feasibility of 3 N-CQDs in the detection of Fe3+ in real water samples, the spike recovery method was used to detect the amount of Fe3+ added in different water samples. The results were shown in Table S5. It could be found that both in tap water or lake water, the recoveries of samples were 97 % to 102 %, and RSDs were less than 5 %. Therefore, this method was suitable for the determination of Fe3+ in real water samples.

The feasibility of the prepared 3 N-CQDs for bioimaging applications was evaluated by cytotoxicity assay. Using human cervical cancer (HeLa) cells as a model, cytotoxicity studies were performed with different concentrations of 3 N-CQDs (0 – 300 μg·mL−1) (Fig. 8b). 3 N-CQDs showed relatively low cytotoxicity, and the viability of HeLa cells remained around 96 % even after 24 h culture at a high concentration of 300 μg·mL−1. The results indicated that the prepared 3 N-CQDs had low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility for HeLa cells, and were suitable fluorescent probes for bioimaging.

Synthesized 3 N-CQDs were added to in vitro cultured HeLa cells to demonstrate their cellular imaging capabilities. The fluorescence of HeLa cells was observed with an inverted fluorescence microscope under the condition of an external wavelength of 365 nm. As shown in Fig. 9 a and 9b, after culturing HeLa cells with the medium containing different concentrations of 3 N-CQDs for 24 h, the cells showed bright blue light with good cell morphology, thus indicating that 3 N-CQDs easily penetrated the cell membrane, entered the cells, and had relatively low cytotoxicity.

Next, 100 μmol·L−1 Hg2+ and 150 μmol·L−1 Fe3+ were mixed with fluorescently-labeled cells cultured with 3 N-CQDs. The cells were then incubated at room temperature for 30 min to explore the effects of Hg2+ and Fe3+ on cell fluorescence imaging. Fig. 9c and 9d show that the fluorescence of cells was significantly quenched after Hg2+ was added to the cultured cells; the cell morphology was good. The fluorescence intensity of cells became significantly weaker when Fe3+ was added to the cultured cells, thus indicating that the synthesized 3 N-CQDs were suitable for biological imaging of live cells by Hg2+ or Fe3+ (Fig. 9e and 9f). The above results showed that the synthesized 3 N-CQDs had good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity, as well as good membrane permeability for HeLa cells. They can perform fluorescent sensing of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in live cells.

3 N-CQDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method through the hydrothermal reaction of CA and 3 N-β-CD at 180 °C for 3 h. This method was efficient, fast, economical, and environmentally friendly. Fluorescence spectroscopy studies showed that the prepared 3 N-CQDs had good water solubility, stability, good fluorescence activity, and high fluorescence quantum yield. Based on the selective and sensitive fluorescence response of 3 N-CQDs to Hg2+ and Fe3+, a novel and efficient fluorescent probe for the detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in an aqueous solution was constructed with detection limits of 0.25 μmol·L−1 and 0.57 μmol·L−1, respectively. It resisted interference from other cations. CCK8 experiments and cellular imaging studies showed that 3 N-CQDs had low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, and membrane permeability for HeLa cells, and could be used for bioimaging of live cells. It offered fluorescence sensing of intracellular Hg2+ and Fe3+. In summary, the carbon quantum dots 3 N-CQDs synthesized here offered simple and fast preparation, strong selectivity, and high sensitivity. It can be used as an ideal fluorescent probe for the detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in water with broad application prospects.

Ju Yang: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Yunhan Yang: Investigation, Methodology. Lijiao Su: Investigation. Xin Tao: Investigation. Juntong Zhang: Investigation. Yan Chen: Investigation. Lijuan Yang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
